title: Land and Water Conservation Fund Water Amendments Act of 2025
short_title: Land and Water Conservation Fund Water Amendments Act of 2025
plain_summary: The Land and Water Conservation Fund Water Amendments Act of 2025 lets states use special government money for cleaning up dirty water in addition to building parks and playgrounds. States must make plans that show which waters are polluted and how they want to fix them. The federal government can help pay for projects that use nature to clean water, like creating wetlands and marshes. This law helps protect both outdoor fun places and clean water for communities.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Land and Water Conservation Fund Water Amendments Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Financial Assistance to States for Water Quality Projects
sections.text: This section changes an existing law about money that goes to states for outdoor recreation. The changes allow states to also use this money for projects that improve water quality. States must include water quality improvement in their outdoor recreation plans. States must identify any polluted bodies of water in their area that need cleanup according to federal water pollution rules. They must also list any proposed water quality projects for these polluted waters. The government can now give money to states for water quality projects. These projects must improve water quality by working with natural water systems like wetlands, marshes, living shorelines, waters near the shore, or other natural water features that help reduce harmful nutrients. States can count money they spend on water quality projects toward the required matching funds they must provide. The Secretary of the Interior must work with the Environmental Protection Agency when giving out this money. The money cannot pay for projects that are already finished or fully paid for. This law does not give the federal government more power over local waters or allow the Secretary to control how water quality projects are done.
